item.title: Authenticity and its discontents
item.description: Simon Frith’s Performing Rites makes a claim that sounds simple and proves difficult to absorb: when a listener calls a record “authentic,” they are not detecting a quality the music possesses but applying a set of learned conventions about what authentic music should sound like.

item.title: Cultural capital
item.description: Two children walk into a museum on a school trip.

item.title: Cultural omnivorousness
item.description: A philosophy professor who keeps a Dolly Parton record next to the Bach on the shelf would have puzzled the sociologist of 1970.

item.title: Pop as craft
item.description: Simon Frith’s Performing Rites makes a deceptively simple observation that reframes the entire debate: if authenticity in popular music is a genre convention rather than a detected quality, then the hierarchy that places raw self-expression above professional craft is a cultural judgment instead of ...

item.title: The color line in pop
item.description: The color line in American popular music is not a metaphor.

item.title: The pop factory
item.description: Theodor Adorno argued in On Popular Music that the culture industry standardizes its products: every hit follows a pre-given pattern, and the variations between individual songs are surface differences designed to mask that uniformity.

item.title: The songwriter-performer divide
item.description: The question of who owns the song — the person who wrote it or the person who sings it — runs through the entire history of popular music.

item.title: The transatlantic feedback loop
item.description: The pattern that defines the transatlantic movement of popular music — Black American musicians creating a form, white British musicians absorbing and re-exporting it, America responding to the British version — has repeated across so many decades that it functions as a permanent circuit.

item.title: Dionne Warwick
item.description: Genres: Pop soul, standards Scene: Brill Building Era: 1962–present (primary period: 1962–1971) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify Listen to the way Dionne Warwick navigates the melody of “Walk on By” — the intervals are wide, and the phrasing syncopates against Burt Bacharach’s rhythm track in ways that m...

item.title: Laura Nyro
item.description: Genres: Pop soul, singer-songwriter, progressive soul Scene: Brill Building (adjacent), New York City Era: 1966–1997 (primary period: 1967–1971) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify The piano on “Timer” (1968) shifts from a ballad’s tenderness to a percussive, almost violent intensity in the space of a breat...
